pages.title: About me
pages.text: The mere formulation of a problem is far more essential than its solution, which may be merely a matter of mathematical or experimental skill. To raise new questions, new possibilities, to regard old problems from a new angle requires creative imagination and marks real advances in science. ​ –— Albert Einstein Wang Xu Ph.D. Candidate, Computer Science City University of Hong Kong My research intresets mainly include the following topics: Information-theoretic approaches to machine learning Representation learning Variational inference and deep generative models

posts.title: Score Matching for Generative Model
posts.text:
Generative Modeling by Estimating Gradients of the Data Distrbution - NIPS 2019 (1) Main Contribution Introduce a new generative model, where samples are produced via Langevin dynamics using gradients of $p(x;	heta)$ with score matching. Since score gradients are hard to estimate in low-dimensional manifolds, the authors perturb the data with gaussian noise and instead estimate the score function of the perturbed data distribution. Annealed Langevin dynamics methods can gradully decrease the noise level as the sampling process gets close to the data manifold. Their methods avoid sampling process during the training. The score function is parameterized with a neural network instead of kernel functions. (2) Background & Motivation Generative models have many applications in machine learning. Basically, we want to train a generative model $p(x;	heta)$ to simulate the sample generating process of $p_{data}(x)$. Recent works are mainly driven by two approaches: likelihood-based methods (e.g. VAE) and generative adversarial networks. Likelihood-based models normally maximize the log-likelihood or the surrogate evidence lower bound, but the difficulty of that has been attributed to the partition function (or named normalization constant). The normalized probability density has to be non-negative and integrated to 1, so the energy-based models or some specialized architectures (e.g. flow models) are widely used in this scenia. Generative adversarial networks have achieved great success in recent few years, but the training can be unstable due to the adversarial objective and often criticized with the mode collapse problem. Noise contrastive estimation and minimum probability flow typically only work well in low-dimensional cases. (3) Proposed methodsNotation: Define the score of a probability density $p(\mathrm{x})$ as $
abla_{\mathrm{x}}\log p(\mathrm{x})$. The score network $S_{	heta}:\mathbb{R}^Dightarrow \mathbb{R}^D$ is a neural network parameterized by $	heta$. Goal: Use the dataset to learn a model for generating new samples from $p_{data}(\mathrm{x})$. (3.1) Score matching for score estimation  egin{align} &	ext{minimize }  rac{1}{2}\mathbb{E}_{p_{data}}[\|S_{	heta}(\mathrm{x})-
abla_{\mathrm{x}}\log p_{data}(\mathrm{x})\|^2_2] \ \Leftrightarrow &	ext{minimize } \mathbb{E}_{p_{data}}[	ext{tr}(
abla_{\mathrm{x}}{S_{	heta}(\mathrm{x})})+  rac{1}{2}\|S_{	heta}(\mathrm{x})\|^2_2] \end{align}Where $
abla_{\mathrm{x}}S_{	heta}(\mathrm{x})$ denotes the Jacobian of $S_{	heta}(\mathrm{x})$, which is not scalable to high dimensional data. Denosing score matching can completely circumvents $	ext{tr}(
abla_{\mathrm{x}}{S_{	heta}(\mathrm{x})})$. It perturbs the data point $\mathrm{x}$ with a pre-specified noise distribution $q_{\delta}(	ilde{\mathrm{x}}|\mathrm{x})$ and instead employing score matching to estimate the perturbed data distribution $q_{\delta}(	ilde{\mathrm{x}})	riangleq \int q_{\delta}(	ilde{\mathrm{x}}|\mathrm{x})p_{data}(\mathrm{x}) d\mathrm{x}$. Then the objective function is:  egin{align}  rac{1}{2} \mathbb{E}_{q_{\delta}(	ilde{\mathrm{x}}|\mathrm{x})p_{data}}[\|S_{	heta}(	ilde{\mathrm{x}})-
abla_{	ilde{\mathrm{x}}}\log q_{\delta}(	ilde{\mathrm{x}}|\mathrm{x})\|^2_2] \end{align}Sliced score matching uses random projections to approximate the trace term in (2). The objective is  egin{align} \mathbb{E}_{p_{\mathrm{v}}}\mathbb{E}_{p_{data}}[\mathrm{v}^T 
abla_{\mathrm{x}}{S_{	heta}(\mathrm{x})}\mathrm{v}+  rac{1}{2}\|S_{	heta}(\mathrm{x})\|^2_2] \end{align}Where $p_{\mathrm{v}}$ is a simle distribution of random vectors. (3.2) Sampling with Langevin dynamicsGiven a fixed step size $\epsilon>0$ and an initial value $	ilde{\mathrm{x}}_0\sim \pi({\mathrm{x}})$ with $\pi$ is a prior distribution, $\mathrm{z}_t \sim \mathcal{N}(0,I)$,  egin{align} 	ilde{\mathrm{x}}_t = 	ilde{\mathrm{x}}_{t-1} +  rac{\epsilon}{2} 
abla_{\mathrm{x}} \log p(	ilde{\mathrm{x}}_{t-1}) + \sqrt{\epsilon}\mathrm{z}_t \end{align}

posts.title: Fisher Inference of Intractable models
posts.text: Title: Fisher Efficient Inference of Intractable Models - NIPS 2019(1) Contributions Contruct a generic inference method by minimizing the KL divergence without the normalization term. (2) Background & Motivation Learning via MLE requires evaluating the normalization term of the density model. Many approaches have been proposed to overcome this problem by approximating the partition function or its gradient, e.g. importance sampling MLE, contrastive divergence, amortized MLE, etc. The above solutions come at large computational cost or complexity, and sometimes will lead to estimation error. The issue of intractable normalization constant can be avoid by minimizing the other divergence, such as fisher divergence (score matching), kernel stein discrepancy (kernel maximum mean discrepancy). We have to carefully choose the kernel in the above kernel-based approaches. (3) Proposed methodsProblem Formulation: Given i.i.d samples $X:=\{x^{(i)}\}_{i=1}^N$, train the probability density model $p(x;\theta)$ close to the true data distribution $p(x)$. MLE: \begin{align} \mathop{\min}_{\theta} D_{\text{KL}}[p|p_{\theta}] &= \mathop{\min}_{\theta} \mathbb{E}[\log \frac{p(x)}{p_{\theta}(x)}] \\ &= C - \mathop{\max}_{\theta} \mathbb{E}[\log p_{\theta}(x)] \end{align}We can see that the objective function for MLE $\mathop{\max}_{\theta} \mathbb{E}[\log p_{\theta}(x)]$ is equivlent to the objective derived from minimizing KL divergence between $p$ and $p_{\theta}$. Here is another approach. We can estimate the density ratio $\frac{p(x)}{p_{\theta}(x)}$ first, and then approximate the KL by taking the average over samples $X$. Then the density model parameter $\theta$ can be subsequently estimated by minimizing the approximation of KL divergence. (3.1) Density ratio estimation frameworkWe can model the density ratio using a function $r(x;\delta)$ parameterized by $\delta$, then we can define $\tilde{p}(x) = r(x;\delta)p_{\theta}(x)$. To estimate the parameter $\delta$, we can minimize the KL divergence $D_{\text{KL}}[p|\tilde{p}]$: \begin{align} &\mathop{\min}_{\delta} D_{\text{KL}}[p|\tilde{p}] \\ & \text{s.t.} \int r(x;\delta)p(x) dx = 1 \end{align} which is equivlent to \begin{align} &\mathop{\min}_{\delta} -\mathbb{E}_p [\log r(x;\delta)] \\ & \text{s.t.} \int r(x;\delta)p_{\theta}(x) dx = 1 \end{align}Note that $-\mathbb{E}_p [\log r(x;\hat{\delta)}]$ is the KL divergence between $p$ and $p_{\theta}$. This optimization problem needs some tricks to solve. For example, we can set $r(x;\delta)$ as the energe function $r(x;\delta)=\frac{e^{f(x;\delta)}}{\mathbb{E}[e^{f(x\delta)}]}$, which has already been normalized. (3.2) Stein Density Ratio EstimationStein Features: Feature function $f(x):\mathbb{R}^d\rightarrow\mathbb{R}^d$; Density model $p(x;\theta):\mathbb{R}^d\rightarrow \mathbb{R}$. Stein operator $\mathcal{A}$ : \mathcal{A}f(x) = +\text{tr}(\nabla_x^2f(x))$\mathcal{A}f(x)\in\mathbb{R}^b$ Is the stein feature $\mathcal{A}f(x):=[\mathcal{A}f_1(x),\dots,\mathcal{A}f_b(x)]^T$ Stein’s Identity If $\mathop{\lim}_{|x|\rightarrow \infty} p(x)\partial_xf(x)=0$ , then $\mathbb{E}_p[\mathcal{A}f(x)]=0$. Define a linear density ratio model: $r(x;\delta):=\delta^T\mathcal{A}f(x)+1$. We can see that this density ratio estimator is self-normalized: $\mathbb{E}[r(x;\delta)]=1$. Therefore, we can solve the following problem: \begin{align} \hat{\delta}&=\mathop{\arg\min}_{\delta\in\mathbb{R}^b} -\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^N \log r_{\theta}(x_i;\delta)+C\\ &= \mathop{\arg\max}_{\delta\in\mathbb{R}^b} \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^N [\log \delta^T\mathcal{A}f(x_i)+1] \end{align}Up to now, we can approximate the KL divergence from $p$ to $p_{\theta}$ with the optimal $\hat{\delta}$. (3.3) Intractable Model InferenceLet $l(\hat{\delta},\theta)=\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^N r_{\theta}(x_i;\hat{\delta)}$ and we use it as a replacement of $D_{\text{KL}}[p||p_{\theta}]$. To estimate the optimal $\theta$, we can solve the following problem: \mathop{\min}_{\theta} l(\hat{\delta},\theta) \Leftrightarrow \mathop{\min}_{\theta}\mathop{\max}_{\delta}l(\delta,\theta)The Lagrangian dual problem of Eq.(8) is \begin{align} &\hat{\mu} = \arg\min_\mu \sum_{i=1}^N[-(\log(-\mu_i)-1)]-\sum_{i=1}^N \mu_i\\ &\text{s.t. } \sum_{i=1}^N \mu_i\mathcal{A}_{\theta}f(x_i)=0 \end{align} Therefore, instead of solving the minmax problem, we can solve the following constrained minimization problem: \begin{align} &\min_\theta\min_\mu \sum_{i=1}^N[-(\log(-\mu_i)-1)]-\sum_{i=1}^N \mu_i\\ &\text{s.t. } \sum_{i=1}^N \mu_i\mathcal{A}_{\theta}f(x_i)=0 \end{align}

posts.title: PyTorch Notes
posts.text: PyTorch 1. Tensor1234import torchtorch.FloatTensor(2, 3)torch.IntTensor(2, 3)x.type() Generate random data: 1234torch.rand(2, 3) # torch.FloatTensor 2*3 uniform distribution [0,1]torch.randn(2, 3) # torch.FloatTensor 2*3 normal N(0,1)torch.range(1, 3) # 1,2,3torch.zeros(2, 3) # torch.FloatTensor 2*3 all 0 Operations: 1234567torch.abs(a)c = torch.add(a, b)torch.clamp(a, -0.1, 0.1) # all elements ranged in [-.1, .1]torch.div(a, b) # element divisiontorch.mul(a, b) # element multiplicationtorch.pow(a, 2) torch.mm(a, b) # matrix multiplication 123456789101112device = torch.device("cuda:0" if torch.cuda.is_available() else "cpu")x = torch.randn(3, 3, dtype=torch.float64, device=device)x = torch.zeros(1, requires_grad=True)data.to(device)model = MyModule(...).to(device)tensor.datacpu_imgs.cuda() # cpu tensor -> gpu tensorgpu_imgs.cpu() # gpu tensor -> cpu tensortorch.from_numpy(imgs) # numpy -> cpu tensorcpu_imgs.numpy() # cpu tensor -> numpy# GPU tensor cannot convert to numpy directly，must set as CPU tensor firstloss_output.item() # if the tensor is a scalar How to reshape the tensor: Change the shape 1234567891011121314import torch# input a = torch.rand(4, 1, 28, 28)print(a.shape)print(a.view(4 * 1, 28, 28).shape)print(a.reshape(4 * 1, 28, 28).shape)print(a.reshape(4, 1 * 28 * 28).shape)print(a.view(-1, 8).shape)# outputtorch.Size([4, 1, 28, 28])torch.Size([4, 28, 28])torch.Size([4, 28, 28])torch.Size([4, 784])torch.Size([392, 8]) Add a dimension: when $i$ in $[0,4]$ and $i>0$, insert one dimension in front of this index $i$. When $i<0$, insert one dimension after this index $i$. 12345678910a = torch.randn(4, 1, 28, 28)print(a.shape)print(a.unsqueeze(0).shape)print(a.unsqueeze(-1).shape)print(a.unsqueeze(-5).shape)# outputtorch.Size([4, 1, 28, 28])torch.Size([1, 4, 1, 28, 28])torch.Size([4, 1, 28, 28, 1])torch.Size([1, 4, 1, 28, 28]) Delete a dimension: Delete the specified dimension with $dim=1$. 12345678910a = torch.Tensor(1, 4, 1, 9)print(a.shape)print(a.squeeze().shape) print(a.squeeze(0).shape)print(a.squeeze(2).shape) # outputtorch.Size([1, 4, 1, 9])torch.Size([4, 9])torch.Size([4, 1, 9])torch.Size([1, 4, 9]) Repeat the dimension: 1234567b = torch.Tensor(1, 32, 1, 1)print(b.shape)b = b.repeat(4, 1, 14, 14)print(b.shape)# outputtorch.Size([1, 32, 1, 1])torch.Size([4, 32, 14, 14]) Transpose the dimension: 12345d = torch.Tensor(6, 3, 1, 2)print(d.transpose(1, 3).shape) # torch.Size([6, 2, 1, 3])a = torch.rand(4, 3, 6, 7)print(a.permute(0, 2, 3, 1).shape) # torch.Size([4, 6, 7, 3]) 2. How to build a NN123456789101112131415161718192021222324252627282930import torch# initial parameters batch_n = 100hidden_unit = 100input_data = 1000output_data = 10epoch_n = 20learning_rate = 1e-6# initial weightsx = torch.randn(batch_n,input_data)y = torch.randn(batch_n,output_data)w1 = torch.randn(input_data,hidden_unit)w2 = torch.randn(hidden_unit,output_data)# Gradient descent; train the modelfor epoch in range(epoch_n): h1 = x.mm(w1) # 100*1000 h1 = h1.clamp(min=0) y_pred = h1.mm(w2) # 100*10 loss = (y_pred - y).pow(2).sum() print("Epoch:{} , Loss:{:.4f}".format(epoch, loss)) gray_y_pred = 2 * (y_pred - y) gray_w2 = h1.t().mm(gray_y_pred) grad_h = gray_y_pred.clone() grad_h = grad_h.mm(w2.t()) grad_h.clamp_(min=0) grad_w1 = x.t().mm(grad_h) w1 -= learning_rate * grad_w1 w2 -= learning_rate * gray_w2 3. Autograd1234567891011121314151617181920212223242526272829303132333435363738394041import torchimport torch.nnfrom torch.autograd import Variableclass Model(nn.Module): def __init__(self): super(Model,self).__init__() def forward(self,input,w1,w2): x = torch.mm(input,w1) x = torch.clamp(x,min=0) x = torch.mm(x,w2) return x # initial parametersbatch_n = 100hidden_layer = 100input_data = 1000output_data = 10 x = Variable(torch.randn(batch_n , input_data) , requires_grad = False)y = Variable(torch.randn(batch_n , output_data) , requires_grad = False) w1 = Variable(torch.randn(input_data,hidden_layer),requires_grad = True)w2 = Variable(torch.randn(hidden_layer,output_data),requires_grad = True) # set train parametersepoch_n = 20learning_rate = 1e-6for epoch in range(epoch_n): y_pred = model(x, w1, w2) loss = (y_pred - y).pow(2).sum() print("Epoch:{} , Loss:{:.4f}".format(epoch, loss.data[0])) loss.backward() w1.data -= learning_rate * w1.grad.data w2.data -= learning_rate * w2.grad.data w1.grad.data.zero_() w2.grad.data.zero_() 4. PyTorch Programming123456789101112131415161718import argparseimport osimport numpy as npimport mathimport itertoolsimport torchvision.transforms as transformsfrom torchvision.utils import save_imagefrom torch.utils.data import DataLoaderfrom torchvision import datasetsfrom torch.autograd import Variableimport torch.nn as nnimport torch.nn.functional as Fimport torchos.makedirs("results/images/", exist_ok=True) 12345678# set the parametersparser = argparse.ArgumentParser()parser.add_argument("--n_epochs", type=int, default=200, help="number of epochs of training")parser.add_argument("--batch_size", type=int, default=64, help="size of the batches")parser.add_argument("--lr", type=float, default=0.0002, help="adam: learning rate")parser.add_argument("--b1", type=float, default=0.5, help="adam: decay of first order momentum of gradient")parser.add_argument("--b2", type=float, default=0.999, help="adam: decay of first order momentum of gradient")opt = parser.parse_args() 4.1 Coding Flow Data Prepare for the train data, test data; input, output; mini-batch Tensor type (CPU or GPU) Model (NN structure) Define the model structure $f: X\rightarrow \hat{Y}$ Loss function, optimizer cross entropy loss … Adam … Training the model Mini-batch training Backward 4.2 Data Processing12345678910111213141516171819# mnist for example# Configure data loaderos.makedirs("../../data/mnist", exist_ok=True)dataloader = torch.utils.data.DataLoader( datasets.MNIST( "../../data/mnist", train=True, download=True, transform=transforms.Compose( [transforms.Resize(opt.img_size), transforms.ToTensor(), transforms.Normalize([0.5], [0.5])] ), ), batch_size=opt.batch_size, shuffle=True,)cuda = True if torch.cuda.is_available() else FalseFloatTensor = torch.cuda.FloatTensor if cuda else torch.FloatTensorLongTensor = torch.cuda.LongTensor if cuda else torch.LongTensor Normally, the framework is: 123dataloader = torch.utils.data.DataLoader(dataset, shuffle=True, batch_size=32)for i, data in enumerate(dataLoader, 0): x, y = data 4.3 Construct the modelNormally, the model is defined as following: 1234567class Model(nn.Module): def __init__(self): super(Model, self).__init__() def forward(self, input) return mymodel = Model() More concretely: 123456789101112class Net(nn.Module): def __init__(self, input_size=2, hidden_size=100, sigma=0.02): super().__init__() self.fc1 = nn.Linear(input_size, hidden_size) self.fc2 = nn.Linear(hidden_size, hidden_size) self.fc3 = nn.Linear(hidden_size, 1) def forward(self, input): output = F.elu(self.fc1(input)) output = F.elu(self.fc2(output)) output = self.fc3(output) return output 12345models = torch.nn.Sequential( torch.nn.Linear(input_data,hidden_layer), torch.nn.ReLU(), torch.nn.Linear(hidden_layer,output_data)) To cuda if GPU is avaliable: 1234model = Net()if cuda: model.cuda() 4.4 Loss function & optimizer12345# Loss functionscriterion = torch.nn.MSELoss()criterion = torch.nn.CrossEntropyLoss()optimizer = torch.optim.Adam(model.parameters(), lr=opt.lr, betas=(opt.b1, opt.b2)) 4.5 Train the model123456789101112for epoch in range(opt.n_epochs): for i, (imgs, labels) in enumerate(dataloader): batch_size = imgs.shape[0] # Configure input X_data = Variable(imgs.type(FloatTensor)) labels = to_categorical(labels.numpy(), num_columns=opt.n_classes) y_pred = model(X_data) loss = criterion(y_pred, labels) optimizer.zero_grad() loss.backward() optimizer.step() 5 How to save the model1234567891011chkpt_name = 'mymodel'+'.pt'torch.save({ 'traindata': traindata, 'MODEL_PATH': model.state_dict() }, chkpt_name)checkpoint = torch.load( chkpt_name, map_location='cuda' if torch.cuda.is_available() else 'cpu')data = checkpoint['traindata']model = checkpoint['MODEL_PATH']

posts.title: python notes
posts.text: python notes Everything in python is object. ObjectWe use the feature (what is it) and behaviors (what it can do) to describe the object. For example, the dog has many features such as color, age, and weight. They can run and bark, which are the normal behaviors of dog. In python, the feature is called attribute, and the behavior is called method. Object = attribute + method ClassThe objects with same attributes and methods are classified as a class. E.g. human, animal and plant. 123class people: def speak(): print('Hello!') Only objects have attributes, not class. We can set attributes for instances. 12dd = people()dd.age = 16 Initialize an objectSelf: Self represents the instance of the class. By using the ‘self’ keyword we can access the attributes and methods of the class in python. It binds the attributes with the given arguments. Instance calling. __init__ : The task of this function is to initialize (assign values) to the data members of the class when an object of class is created. Like methods, a constructor also contains collection of statements (i.e. instructions) that are executed at time of Object creation. It is run as soon as an object of a class is instantiated. The method is useful to do any initialization you want to do with your object. __str__: It tells python the content when it is required to print an object. __call__: Regard the object as a function. This method can change the object state. 12345678910111213141516171819202122class people: def __init__(self, name, age): self.age = age self.name = name def __str__(self): return ('My name is {}, and my age is {}'.format(self.name, self.age)) def __call__(self, name, age): self.age = age self.name = name print('My name is {}'.format(self.name)) def speak(self): print('My name is {}'.format(self.name)) baby = people('Wendy', 16)baby.speak() # My name is Wendybaby.age # 16print(baby) # My name is Wendy, and my age is 16baby('Eddie', 18) # My name is Eddiebaby.age # 18 Example123456789101112131415161718192021222324class point(): def __init__(self, x=0, y=0): self.x = x self.y = y def get_x(self): return self.x def get_y(self): return self.yclass line(): def __init__(self, p1, p2): self.x = p1.get_x() - p2.get_x() self.y = p1.get_y() - p2.get_y() self.len = (self.x**2 + self.y**2)**(0.5) def get_len(self): return self.lenp1 = point(0, 0)p2 = point(3, 4)Line = line(p1, p2)Line.get_len() # 5

posts.title: Variational AutoEncoder
posts.text: The reconstruction errorThe reconstruction loss for VAE is normally written as: L_{rec} = \mathbb{E}_{q_{\phi}(\mathrm{z}|\mathrm{x})}[\log p_{\theta}(\mathrm{x}|\mathrm{z})] \tag{1}The recognition model $q_{\phi}(\mathrm{z}|\mathrm{x})$ is a probabilistic encoder: given a datapoint $\mathrm{x}$, it produces a distribution over the possible values of the code $\mathrm{z}$. We refer $p_{\theta}(\mathrm{x}|\mathrm{z})$ as a probabilistic decoder: given a code $\mathrm{z}$, it produces a distribution over the possible corresponding values of $\mathrm{x}$. Normally, we can assume that \mathrm{z}|\mathrm{x} = \mathcal{N}(\mu_{\mathrm{z}|\mathrm{x}},\Sigma_{\mathrm{z}|\mathrm{x}}) \tag{2}For VAEs implemented in practice, people often represent the reconstruction loss as the L2 loss between each $\mathrm{x}$ (training image) and $\hat{\mathrm{x}}$ (decoded image). WHY? Typically in VAE implementations, we can also assume that \mathrm{x}|\mathrm{z} = \mathcal{N}(\mu_{\mathrm{x}|\mathrm{z}},\Sigma_{\mathrm{x}|\mathrm{z}}) \tag{3}Therefore, the output of the decoder is actually follow a Gaussian distribution with mean $\mu_{\mathrm{x}|\mathrm{z}}$ and a unitary covariance. Then we have \log p_{\theta}(\mathrm{x}|\mathrm{z}) = -\frac{1}{2}[\log |\Sigma|+k\log 2\pi+(\mathrm{x}-\mu)^T(\mathrm{x}-\mu)]We can see that since the first two terms are constant with respect to $\mu$, the optimization problem is equivalent to maximize $-\frac{1}{2}(\mathrm{x}-\mu)^T(\mathrm{x}-\mu)$, which is the just the L2 loss between $\mathrm{x}$ and $\mu$. We can approximate the expectation by sample average.
